item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The japanese title confused me; it sounds too much like it's saying "looks like a mother" in my opinion. In the way you're using the english "I want to see my mother", wouldn't "see" be closer in meaning to something like "meet" than "look upon"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Vetoing it. While it is an interesting parallel, 2457-A is one entity. It is vastly different from the seven humans merged into one (see SCP-2478).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Reading this article reminded me of another anomaly, in that it appeared to be an inverse of the other: while the babies in 2457 are split apart by seven, the humans in 2478 are multiplied by seven.

As such, editing:

SCP-2457-A will remain unburnt during the combustion of the host, but it will inevitably expire following the host's expiration due to interruption of gestation. In addition, following the host's expiration, SCP-2457-A's carcass is split into seven portions: head, left and right arms, left and right legs, upper and lower torso (cut off at waistline). Cause is unknown, although wounds on SCP-2457 suggest the application of a bladed object on the carcass.

To:

SCP-2457-A will remain unburnt during the combustion of the host, but it will inevitably expire following the host's expiration due to interruption of gestation. In addition, following the host's expiration, SCP-2457-A's carcass is split into seven portions: head, left and right arms, left and right legs, upper and lower torso (cut off at waistline). Cause is unknown, although wounds on SCP-2457 suggest the application of a bladed object on the carcass.

If anyone has any other ideas, feel free to suggest them below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Utterly believable and fascinating. I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
put it below your autopsy part.

I am afraid I will reject this suggestion. I would rather go by chronology.

I may or may not consider reviewing the interview, but I personally regard it to be sufficient in length.

The word "sensei" seems really awkward in your interview log. If they were talking in English, why sensei?

I will note that I clearly stated that the interview was originally in Japanese. It is only presented here in English because this is an English site that uses English and we cater to English literates here. In my mind, the Foundation would have the original interview in Japanese and translate it into English. Some terms may be kept to prevent them from being lost in translation, which might change the nuances of the statements entirely.

I use "sensei" as it is a conventional term to term to a priest (at least from Google). It is mostly like calling a Protestant priest "Father". I could translate it as "Father" or "priest", but that would feel utterly awkward and imply Christianity instead (which is incorrect).

Mind you that "sensei" is not necessarily "teacher", but it is a broader term for people worthy of some form of respect in society. In English, we don't really have a precise word to express that concept (or at least something that is concise enough to be spoken in conversation).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview log is not working for me right now; Everything else looks pretty fine together. I would suggest making it shorter, or put it below your autopsy part.

The word "sensei" seems really awkward in your interview log. If they were talking in English, why sensei? If the conversation was in Japanese originally then got translated into English, still doesn't make sense why the agent would call the interviewee "sensei".

Seems like you only used the word "sensei" to remind your readers that this happened in Japan.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Ah, that would explain why it was still a hotlink to the sandbox. Anyway, I wouldn't really worry about that, if it doesn't stay afloat, the files will disappear when the article is deleted.

And thanks for keeping the file size in mind. All that data is downloaded to every cellphone and tablet on a mobile data plan, so every little bit of bandwidth saving counts. ;)

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for localising them. I usually would wait until I am certain that the article can keep afloat before I settle the files and mainlist nickname.

I will also take note of the part about the >1MB.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: MrWrong, I'm going to assume you forgot to change the image component to link to a local file. I fixed that. However, please don't use images over 1 MB in size if you don't have to. In this case, resizing it to the standard 300px width drops the filesize to 62 kB and doesn't make any difference in terms of presentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I saw this in the forums a while back. Anyway, I think this is a good mixture of biology and Japanese mythology, which is interesting even by itself. +1

item.title: Thanks, Attribution and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
This has been sitting in my sandbox for a while. Originally, I wanted it to be for Mother's Day… but the theme deviated from the Mother's Day message as I wrote and edit it with feedback.

So yes, I thank the following individuals among others: Hercules Rockefeller, minmin, WrongJohnSilver, TL333s, ignorant_genius among others. The title for this work (母を見たい)1 is credited to a RL associate of mine, who kindly aided me in the Japanese.

Image credits are as follows:

  • Image 1 by Bobjgalindo of Wikimedia (https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=5654847) under GFDL
  • Image 2 by Nisreen M Khalifa et al of Wikimedia (https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fetus_in_fetu_radiograph.jpg) under CC 2.0
+ show block
– hide block

SCP-2457-A and the mass of tissues are whatever's left of PoI-2457-01's unborn twin a la fetus in fetu. Either the unborn twin manifested its anomalous potential or the anomalous capacity of PoI-2457-01 that held SCP-2457-A back is losing effectiveness.

But from PoI-2457-01's POV, he rationalised the whole ordeal as a religious experience - using terms and concepts known to him as a Shinto priest.

Footnotes
1. Japanese; "(I) want to see (my) mother"
